---
title: "Ex America bona quaedam"
date: "2026-03-04"
categories: ["Music", "Pavel Haas Qt"]
---


<figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter size-large is-resized" style="margin-top:var(--wp--preset--spacing--0);margin-bottom:var(--wp--preset--spacing--50)"><a href="https://www.logicmatters.net/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/PHQDana4-scaled.jpg"><img src="../images/2026/phqdana4-1024x663-jpg.jpg" alt="" class="wp-image-19097" style="aspect-ratio:1.544522161417751;width:666px;height:auto"/></a></figure>



<p><em>Some</em> good things come out of America -- Dvořák’s String Quintet Op. 97, for example, written while he was staying with a Czech-speaking community in Iowa for the summer of 1893.</p>



<p>And there was a quite joyous performance a few days ago at Wigmore Hall by the Pavel Haas Quartet, joined by that wonderful viola player, their “dear friend” Dana Zemstov. </p>



<p><a href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bmZRZCxc-Ck">There is a video of the whole concert here</a>. Before the interval, the PHQ played Suk's Meditation on an old Bohemian Chorale&nbsp;and Janáček’s String Quartet No. 2 ‘Intimate Letters’ (terrific, of course). But the Dvořák was outstanding -- and a needed reminder of beauty in this world.</p>



<p>(Peter Jarůšek broke a cello string early in the first movement of the Quintet; the performance resumes at 58.45 -- a great place to start watching if you just have half an hour.)</p>



<hr class="wp-block-separator has-alpha-channel-opacity is-style-wide"/>



<p>As chance would have it, the day before the Wigmore Hall concert, <a href="https://www.bbc.co.uk/sounds/play/m002rrym">the BBC Radio 3 Record Review programme was surveying recordings of the Dvořák Quintet Op.97 in its ‘Building a Library’ series</a> (starting at 1.00.15). It is interesting to hear excerpts from some contrasting performances but, unsurprisingly, the recommended recording was the much lauded one made a few years ago by the PHQ joined by their original founder violist Pavel Nikl.  </p>
